questions.question:
An Internet Service Provider (ISP) has the following chunk of CIDR-based IP addresses available with it: 245.248.128.0/20. The ISP wants to give half of this chunk of addresses to Organization A, and a quarter to Organization B, while retaining the remaining with itself. Which of the following is a valid allocation of addresses to A and B?

A. 245.248.136.0/21 and 245.248.128.0/22
B. 245.248.128.0/21 and 245.248.128.0/22
C. 245.248.132.0/22 and 245.248.132.0/21
D. 245.248.136.0/24 and 245.248.132.0/21
questions.to_find: Valid allocation giving half (prefix /21) to Org A and quarter (prefix /22) to Org B from 245.248.128.0/20

understand.plain: An ISP has a /20 block (4096 addresses). Half means 2048 addresses (/21 prefix) and quarter means 1024 addresses (/22 prefix). We need to find allocations where both sub-blocks fall entirely within the original /20 block and do not overlap. The address 245.248.128.0/20 covers 245.248.128.0 to 245.248.143.255.

keywords.explain: The /n notation where n is the number of bits in the network part; a /20 block has 2^(32-20)=4096 addresses.
keywords.example: 245.248.128.0/20 means the first 20 bits are fixed, giving a block of 4096 addresses from 245.248.128.0 to 245.248.143.255.

keywords.explain: Dividing a larger address block into smaller contiguous blocks that are contained within the parent block and are properly aligned (block start must be a multiple of the block size).
keywords.example: Splitting /20 (4096 addresses) into a /21 (2048) and a /22 (1024) sub-blocks.

understand.visual_svg: <svg width='520' height='180' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='10' y='20' width='500' height='40' class='bg'/><text x='260' y='46' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>245.248.128.0/20 (128.0 to 143.255, 4096 addrs)</text><rect x='10' y='80' width='250' height='36' class='hi'/><text x='135' y='104' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>245.248.128.0/21 (Org B /22)</text><rect x='260' y='80' width='250' height='36' style='fill:#D0EDD4;stroke:#1D9E75;stroke-width:2;'/><text x='385' y='104' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>245.248.136.0/21 (Org A: half)</text><rect x='260' y='122' width='125' height='28' style='fill:#FDDDC7;stroke:#D85A30;stroke-width:2;'/><text x='322' y='141' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>245.248.136.0/22 (Org B: quarter)</text><rect x='385' y='122' width='125' height='28' class='bg'/><text x='448' y='141' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>245.248.140.0/22 (ISP retains)</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: The /20 block spans 245.248.128.0 to 245.248.143.255. The first /21 (128.0-135.255) is retained and used for B. The second /21 (136.0-143.255) goes to A. The first /22 of that second half (136.0-139.255) can be carved for B, but in option A, B gets 245.248.128.0/22 (first quarter) and A gets 245.248.136.0/21 (second half).

given.aim: Find valid non-overlapping sub-allocations of /21 (half) to A and /22 (quarter) to B, both within 245.248.128.0/20.

terms.meaning: ISP's parent block; covers .128.0 to .143.255 in the 245.248.x.x space (4096 addresses).
terms.connects: All allocations must lie within this range.

terms.meaning: 2048 addresses; prefix extends one bit beyond /20. Two possible /21 blocks within /20: 128.0/21 (128-135) and 136.0/21 (136-143).
terms.connects: The /21 block for A must be one of these two aligned blocks.
The /22 block for B must not overlap with A's /21 block.

given.plan: Check each option: verify the /21 sub-block is properly aligned within the /20 parent, verify the /22 sub-block is also within the /20 parent, and verify they do not overlap each other.

steps.title: Identify the parent block range
steps.apply: 245.248.128.0/20: 3rd octet fixed bits = 128 (binary 10000000). Block spans 3rd octet 128 to 143. So full range: 245.248.128.0 to 245.248.143.255.
steps.note: Any valid sub-block must have its 3rd octet in range 128-143.

steps.title: Evaluate Option A: A=245.248.136.0/21, B=245.248.128.0/22
steps.apply:
245.248.136.0/21: 3rd octet 136 to 143 (2048 addrs). Check alignment: 136 = 128+8, and /21 blocks are aligned on multiples of 8 in the 3rd octet starting from the /20 boundary (128). 136-128=8=2^3, valid. It is within 128-143. OK.
245.248.128.0/22: 3rd octet 128 to 131 (1024 addrs). Alignment: 128 is multiple of 4, valid. Within 128-143. OK.
Overlap check: A covers 136-143, B covers 128-131. No overlap. ISP retains 132-135 (/22) and 140-143 portion would be... actually ISP retains the /22 block 132.0/22. Valid allocation.
steps.note: Option A is the only one where both blocks are aligned, within the /20, and non-overlapping.

steps.title: Eliminate other options
steps.apply:
Option B: 245.248.128.0/21 (A) and 245.248.128.0/22 (B) — B is a subset of A, they overlap. Invalid.
Option C: 245.248.132.0/22 as A's block and 245.248.132.0/21 as B — C has /22 for A and /21 for B which reverses the size requirement (A should get half=/21). Also overlapping start. Invalid.
Option D: 245.248.136.0/24 — only 256 addresses, not half the block. Invalid.
steps.note: Only Option A satisfies all constraints: correct sizes, proper alignment, within parent, non-overlapping.

questions.formula_note: CIDR subnetting: a /n block of size 2^(32-n) must be aligned to a boundary that is a multiple of its size; sub-blocks must lie entirely within the parent block and must not overlap.

questions.question:
Consider the following routing table at an IP router:

Network No   | Net Mask       | Next Hop
128.96.170.0 | 255.255.254.0  | Interface 0
128.96.168.0 | 255.255.254.0  | Interface 1
128.96.166.0 | 255.255.254.0  | R2
128.96.164.0 | 255.255.252.0  | R3
0.0.0.0      | 0.0.0.0        | R4

For each IP address in Group I, identify the correct choice of the next hop from Group II using the entries from the routing table above.

Group I                  | Group II
i)  128.96.171.92        | a) Interface 0
ii) 128.96.167.151       | b) Interface 1
iii)128.96.163.151       | c) R2
iv) 128.96.164.121       | d) R3
                         | e) R4

A. i-a, ii-c, iii-e, iv-d
B. i-a, ii-d, iii-b, iv-e
C. i-b, ii-c, iii-d, iv-e
D. i-b, ii-c, iii-e, iv-d
questions.to_find: Match each destination IP address to its next hop using the longest prefix match in the routing table

understand.plain: A router has a routing table with network entries, their subnet masks, and next hops. For each destination IP, we apply each subnet mask to both the destination and the network address; if they match, that route is a candidate. Among all matching entries, the one with the longest (most specific) mask is chosen.

keywords.explain: When multiple routing table entries match a destination IP, the router picks the entry whose network mask has the most 1-bits (most specific match).
keywords.example: If both /24 and /22 entries match, the router uses the /24 entry.

keywords.explain: Apply: (Destination IP) AND (Net Mask) — if result equals Network Number, that entry matches.
keywords.example: 128.96.171.92 AND 255.255.254.0 = 128.96.170.0, which matches the first entry.

understand.visual_alt: A routing table with 5 entries; each destination is ANDed with each mask and compared to the network number to find matching routes.

given.aim: Find the next hop for each of the four destination addresses using longest prefix match.

terms.meaning: Covers 2 consecutive /24 networks; the 3rd octet is ANDed with 254 (11111110 in binary), masking the lowest bit.
terms.connects: Determines whether destination's 3rd octet (with bit0 zeroed) matches the network's 3rd octet.

terms.meaning: Covers 4 consecutive /24 networks; the 3rd octet is ANDed with 252 (11111100 in binary), masking the lowest 2 bits.
terms.connects: A broader match — matches more destination addresses.

terms.meaning: Matches every IP address; used when no more-specific route matches.
terms.example: Any IP not matched by the other four entries goes to R4.

given.plan: For each destination IP, AND it with each mask and compare to the corresponding network number. Use the matching entry with the longest (most bits) mask.

steps.title: Route lookup for ii) 128.96.167.151
steps.note: /23 is more specific than /22, so R2 wins over R3.

steps.title: Route lookup for iii) 128.96.163.151
steps.note: 163 AND 252 = 160, not 164, so the /22 entry doesn't match either.

steps.title: Route lookup for iv) 128.96.164.121
steps.note: 164 AND 254 = 164 (since bit 0 of 164 is already 0), but there's no /23 entry for 128.96.164.0 in the table.

solution.result: i-a (Interface 0), ii-c (R2), iii-e (R4), iv-d (R3) => A. i-a, ii-c, iii-e, iv-d

questions.formula_note: IP routing uses longest prefix match: apply each routing table mask to the destination address and compare to the network number; the entry with the most 1-bits in the mask that still matches wins.

questions.question: In the network 200.10.11.144/27, the fourth octet (in decimal) of the last address of the network which can be assigned to a host is _______.
questions.to_find: The value of the 4th octet of the last host-assignable address in the /27 network containing 200.10.11.144

understand.plain: A /27 subnet has a block size of 32 addresses. The subnet mask is 255.255.255.224. To find which /27 block contains .144, we round down to the nearest multiple of 32: floor(144/32)*32 = 4*32 = 128. So the network starts at .128. The last address (.159) is broadcast; the last usable host is .158.

keywords.meaning: 27-bit prefix; 5 host bits; block size = 2^5 = 32. Subnet mask = 255.255.255.224 (last octet = 11100000 in binary).
The network address of a /27 block must be a multiple of 32. The block containing .144 starts at floor(144/32)*32 = 128.

understand.visual_svg: <svg width='440' height='120' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='10' y='20' width='60' height='70' style='fill:#E8DDF5;stroke:#7F77DD;stroke-width:2;'/><text x='40' y='52' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>.128</text><text x='40' y='70' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>Net Addr</text><rect x='70' y='20' width='290' height='70' style='fill:#D0EDD4;stroke:#1D9E75;stroke-width:2;'/><text x='215' y='48' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>.129 to .158</text><text x='215' y='70' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>30 usable host addresses (.144 is in here)</text><rect x='360' y='20' width='70' height='70' style='fill:#FDDDC7;stroke:#D85A30;stroke-width:2;'/><text x='395' y='52' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>.159</text><text x='395' y='70' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>Broadcast</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: The /27 block containing .144 spans 200.10.11.128 (network) to 200.10.11.159 (broadcast). Usable hosts: .129 to .158. Address .144 is a valid host in this range. The last usable host is .158.

given.aim: Find the 4th octet of the last host address in the /27 subnet that contains 200.10.11.144.

terms.meaning: An IP address with /27 prefix. We need to find which /27 block this address belongs to.
terms.connects: Apply mask 255.255.255.224 to find network address.

terms.meaning: 144 AND 224 = 128. So the block starts at 200.10.11.128.

given.plan: Apply mask 255.255.255.224 to .144 to get the network address .128. Compute broadcast = .128 + 31 = .159. Last host = .158.

steps.title: Find the network address
steps.formula_raw: Network address = IP AND Subnet Mask
steps.note: 144 is in the block 128-159 (the 5th /27 block: 4*32=128 to 5*32-1=159).

steps.title: Find broadcast and last host
steps.formula_raw: Broadcast = Network + block_size - 1; Last host = Broadcast - 1
steps.apply:
Block size = 2^5 = 32.
Broadcast = 128 + 32 - 1 = 159. So broadcast = 200.10.11.159.
Last host = 200.10.11.158.
4th octet of last host = 158.

solution.result: The fourth octet of the last host-assignable address is 158.

questions.formula_note: To find the /27 network containing a given IP: apply mask 255.255.255.224 (= IP AND 224 for last octet). Last host = network address + 30 (since block size = 32, broadcast = +31, last host = +30).

questions.question:
Consider three machines M, N, and P with IP addresses 200.10.5.2, 200.10.5.5, and 200.10.5.202 respectively. The subnet mask is set to 255.255.255.252 for all the three machines. Which one of the following is true?

A. M, N, and P all belong to the same subnet
B. Only M and N belong to the same subnet
C. Only N and P belong to the same subnet
D. M, N, and P belong to three different subnets
questions.answer: D. M, N, and P belong to three different subnets
questions.to_find: Determine which of the three machines share the same subnet under mask 255.255.255.252

understand.plain: The mask 255.255.255.252 is a /30, which means only the last 2 bits of the 4th octet are host bits, and each subnet has exactly 4 addresses (2 usable hosts). We AND each IP's 4th octet with 252 to find its subnet. If the results differ, the machines are on different subnets.

keywords.explain: The last octet in binary: 11111100. Only the last 2 bits vary. Each subnet block has size 4 (2^2). Subnet boundaries are multiples of 4: 0, 4, 8, ..., 200, 204, ...
Apply the mask: IP AND Mask. The result is the network address. Two hosts are in the same subnet iff their network addresses match.

understand.visual_svg: <svg width='460' height='160' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='10' y='10' width='140' height='130' class='hi'/><text x='80' y='35' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>M: .5.2</text><text x='80' y='58' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>2 AND 252 = 0</text><text x='80' y='78' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>Subnet: .5.0/30</text><text x='80' y='98' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>(.0 - .3)</text><rect x='160' y='10' width='140' height='130' style='fill:#D0EDD4;stroke:#1D9E75;stroke-width:2;'/><text x='230' y='35' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>N: .5.5</text><text x='230' y='58' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>5 AND 252 = 4</text><text x='230' y='78' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>Subnet: .5.4/30</text><text x='230' y='98' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>(.4 - .7)</text><rect x='310' y='10' width='140' height='130' style='fill:#FDDDC7;stroke:#D85A30;stroke-width:2;'/><text x='380' y='35' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>P: .5.202</text><text x='380' y='58' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>202 AND 252=200</text><text x='380' y='78' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>Subnet: .5.200/30</text><text x='380' y='98' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>(.200-.203)</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Three separate /30 subnets: M in .5.0/30 (addresses .0-.3), N in .5.4/30 (addresses .4-.7), P in .5.200/30 (addresses .200-.203). All three are on different subnets.

given.aim: Determine subnet membership of M(.5.2), N(.5.5), and P(.5.202) under mask 255.255.255.252.

terms.meaning: 4th octet = 2. Apply mask: 2 AND 252 = 0. Network address = 200.10.5.0.
4th octet = 5. Apply mask: 5 AND 252 = 4. Network address = 200.10.5.4.
4th octet = 202. Apply mask: 202 AND 252 = 200. Network address = 200.10.5.200.

given.plan: Compute (4th octet) AND 252 for each machine. Three different results mean three different subnets.

steps.title: Compute subnet ID for each machine
steps.note: Each machine has a different subnet ID: .0, .4, and .200 respectively.

steps.title: Compare subnet IDs
steps.apply:
M's subnet: 200.10.5.0/30 (covers .0 to .3)
N's subnet: 200.10.5.4/30 (covers .4 to .7)
P's subnet: 200.10.5.200/30 (covers .200 to .203)
All three subnet IDs are different. Therefore M, N, and P are all on different subnets.
steps.note: With /30 mask, every group of 4 consecutive IPs forms a subnet. .2, .5, and .202 fall in groups starting at .0, .4, and .200 respectively.

solution.result: D. M, N, and P belong to three different subnets

questions.formula_note: Subnet determination: apply the subnet mask via bitwise AND to the IP address; machines with identical results share a subnet. With /30 (mask 252 in last octet), each subnet block holds only 4 addresses.
